text: This thirty two years female on steroid treatment for chronic ITP for two years presented with falling platelet counts and particular hemorrhage. Her current platelet counts were only ten thousand. Preoperative platelet transfusion with intact spleen is of no help nor do we employ IgG. However, we give vaccination to these patients two weeks before the surgery in non-emergent situations.

The operating room layout is shown with the surgeon standing to the right of the patient, the monitor positioned at the left shoulder level, and a sandbag placed under the left scapula to achieve a thirty-degree tilt. The port positions are detailed: a ten millimeter port above and to the left of the umbilicus for the telescope, a five millimeter port in the epigastrium, and a ten millimeter port in the left midclavicular line for the surgeon's hands. The assistant uses a five millimeter port in the left axillary line. The abdomen shows multiple petechial spots. The importance of identifying and removing accessory spleens during splenectomy is emphasized, as they can lead to recurrent symptoms if not removed.

In the left upper quadrant, the surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure of the colon by dividing the splenocolic ligament. The gastro-splenic ligament is divided to enter the lesser sac, extending upwards to the upper pole of the spleen for better access. The splenic artery is identified by its pulsations, while the lesser sac is further opened to reveal the tail of the pancreas. The splenic vein appears first during dissection, but the surgeon resists dividing it before addressing the splenic artery, which is lifted with an atraumatic grasper for circumferential dissection.

The surgeon creates space to separate the splenic artery from the splenic vein. The splenic artery is lifted and clipped after a reasonable length is dissected. The surface of the spleen shows segmental devascularization, with the upper part appearing dark while the lower part remains perfused. The lower polar splenic artery and vein are identified and dissected, resulting in the entire spleen becoming dark and devascularized.

The surgeon uses harmonic shears to divide the fat around the tail of the pancreas, pushing it away from the splenic hilum for further dissection. A small tributary of the splenic vein from the pancreas is identified and managed. The splenic vein is clipped on the patient's side but remains undivided. Remaining areolar tissues in the splenic hilum are divided, and the posterior leaf of the linonear ligament near the lower part of the spleen is divided, continuing upwards. The division of the linonear ligament is performed from the anterior surface.

The spleen is attached only by the splenic vein, which is divided without clipping the splenic side, causing dark blood to bleed and shrink the spleen for easier delivery. The freed spleen is placed in a plastic bag within the peritoneal cavity, fractured into small pieces, and removed through the midclavicular port. The operative area is inspected, including the posterior leaf of the linoleum ligament and the splenic pedicle, with visible branches of the arteries. The delivery port is closed with transfacial sutures to prevent hernia formation due to steroid use, and the skin wounds are sutured.

The surgeon carefully divides both leaves of the gastro-splenic ligament using harmonic shears, while ensuring the branches of the splenic vein are not damaged. The tail of the pancreas, which is closely positioned to the splenic hilum, is gently pushed away to create space for further dissection. The anchoring branch of the vein is either clipped or coagulated at multiple points to prevent bleeding. Despite the friable tissue due to long-term steroid use, the surgical field remains dry, and any unexpected bleeding is managed by pressing the area with a suction or instrument and using an atraumatic soft grasper to occlude the bleeding. Suturing is performed with one hand due to the left hand being occupied with controlling the bleeding.

With experience even massive spleens can be removed by laparoscopy. This is a one point eight five kilogram spleen which we remove by laparoscopy. The positions of the ports have to be individualized and one must remember that these large splenic veins are not only thin walled but very fragile and bleed very easily on touch so they should be very carefully handled. Laparoscopic spleen acne is the gold standard for patients with chronic ITP. Other hematological disorders with spleenomegaly also can be dealt with by laparoscopy. Hyperspleenism with very massive spleen. Laparoscopic spleen acne is feasible, but complications are higher. Laparoscopic spleen acne has also been reported for large spleens with portal hypertension.
